sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Persistent dizziness may reflect vestibular hypofunction, sensory mismatch, anxiety, migraine, cervicogenic factors, or unresolved central pathology. Rehabilitation is appropriate only after dangerous causes are adequately considered.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Ongoing dizziness, imbalance, or motion sensitivity
- Visual dependence or symptom flare with busy environments
- Reduced confidence walking, turning, or driving
- Nausea, disequilibrium, or exercise intolerance

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Escalate for new neurological signs, acute hearing loss, severe headache, syncope, or a progressive unexplained pattern
- Persistent dizziness often has multifactorial drivers
- Exercise should provoke manageable symptoms, not unsafe crashes

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen falls risk, neuro red flags, and whether the patient can mobilise safely today
- Give simple safety advice about transfers, stairs, and supervision if balance is poor
- Escalate if the pattern is evolving, central, or medically unexplained

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Assess vestibular, visual, cervical, and anxiety-related contributors where appropriate
- Use graded gaze stability, habituation, balance, and walking progression when the diagnosis is suitable
- Coordinate medical, ENT, neurology, or psychology review when symptoms remain atypical or refractory

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - The right vestibular dose is enough to stimulate adaptation without making the patient fear movement more
